author: @kezzatries
text:
As an electrical apprentice in the early 70s,  I had to make insulation tubes out of brown paper and fenolic resin,  we also used a type of varnish to make these tubes.  They were used to make formers to make transformers, coils etc.  We made them on split timber mandrels, that could be collapsed slightly to remove them. 
They were very strong and machinable.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there are all kinds of resins for all kinds of jobs mate - paper tech  is an awesome tech

author: @NorthernKitty
text: I had a knock on my door yesterday.  A forestry worker was doing a regional study and asked if he could enter my property to take measurements of an invasive species growing in my yard.  I asked him what the invasive species was, and he replied "it's a knotweed that people commonly refer to as 'Japanese Bamboo' ".  I told him, sure, he's more than welcome to measure it, if he wouldn't mind bringing me a few stalks as I'd like to make an e-scooter.  He looked at me quite puzzled and didn't seem to get the joke, but it gave me the giggle-fits for a few good minutes.  😋

replies.author: @bigsteve6729
replies.text: Better hope it's not your property else you aren't selling it with knotweed 😂

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - I can imagine it right now lol

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: @bigsteve6729 I'm actually OK - the area where it's growing is on a railroad easement adjacent to my lot that's owned by the township.  Ultimately, they're responsible for it.  😊  But the forestry worker had no way of knowing that, and I didn't know exactly where he was talking about until he was measuring.

author: @krzysztofniedoba1905
text: Dear Mr. Murray-Smith, thanks! I'm not sure, but if the paper is anisotropic, aligning the wood fibers along the axis of the rod should provide you with some extra strength. But even more important, can a pipe be done? It can also be stronger than a rod and you save the precious material  :)

author: @McRootbeer
text: Is there a bio resin/plastic you could use to waterproof the rolls?  Like the hemp plastic Robert made before, thinned to the consistency of water so it can soak into the paper.  Does anyone know of anything like that?

replies.author: @johnmcfadden9336
replies.text:
The magnesium based cements might work well in some applications as they bond very well with cellulose 
Rob has done videos on casein based plastics as well as mag cements

replies.author: @McRootbeer
replies.text: @johnmcfadden9336 I've seen the videos.  Didn't know the cements bonded with paper.  I'd think that concrete would be brittle, but. who knows.  The bioplastic from milk would be what I'd want to try first, the only question is if it can be thinned to soak into the paper/be easier to work with.

replies.author: @johnmcfadden9336
@michaelcorbidge7914
replies.text: The pl asnt oils perhaps.  The well known  one is linseed which also was a basis for paints . C a nola oil  was once used for p a ints also. Cotton  seed oil just 6gats very sticky .

replies.text: milk based - casein - would work very well here mate

author: @McRootbeer
text: This looks just like the roller system they use to make cylindrical cells. replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925

author: @theinsider8872
text: Hey Robert, love your creative projects.  Though I think myself and others would very much appreciate if you revisited all carbon and paper batteries.  I really hope you haven't given up on these things, as they are truly amazing.  If you are moving on from these, perhaps sharing more details and tutorials on how to replicate these would be amazing, such as achieving high electrical conductivity in carbon based inks.   Anyhow, thanks so much for the amazing content!

replies.author: @William_Hada
replies.text: I find that all his videos have more than enough information to get you on your way to serious battery experimentation and development. He gives plenty of details and suggestions for improvement. I think that one of the goals of his channel is to give you inspiration, encouragement, and enough knowledge to experiment on your own. I think he has done that quite well. Specifically, I think you should rewatch his videos because he has mentioned how to achieve higher electrical conductivity in producing your own carbons as well as the best materials to use in conductive inks.

replies.author: @theinsider8872
replies.text: @William_Hada Of course!  I just want to see some more battery stuff.  The more details, the better.  :)  As well some of the older videos have pretty poor camera quality, so much so you can't even see what's happening.  I think it would be great to revisit some battery builds!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have done all of that mate - you j just need to rewatch the older vids - to be honest if all I did was repeat old stuff the channel would soon die a death - it's why I moved onto new stuff as a breath of fresh air all the old stuff is still there and I still work on it - when there I something I new I post it - but to be honest mate -  it really would be just more of the same

replies.author: @theinsider8872
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering For sure.  Would be nice to get an update on those batteries though.  I'm sure many people would buy those in a heart beat.  Ps, what exactly would happen if you were to just manufacture them yourself and sell them on your website?  Is that legal?  Or are you more concerned about someone stealing the tech without all the proper patents?

replies.author: @ambersmith6517
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  this video is about his paper batt and so much more he is showing you any one can do it no matter income or skill you just need to go tinker around
